title: Mini Review for Physical Geography (Part 1)
text:
Mini Review for Physical Geography (Geog 1110)
Submitted by Dr. Cassandra Hansen
1. Describe annual progress / changes / events in the program.
Over the last two semesters the average retention rate has been 91% for Physical
Geography. Physical Geography maintained a steady attendance of dedicated students that
completed this course with success, while some students changed their majors to Geography.
Enrollment in Physical Geography has increased over the last four years, and the course is
already full for spring 2019 with a robust waiting list. New instructional approaches this year
include using the first ten minutes of the class to focus on relevant global physical geographic
events such as: hurricanes, fires or earthquakes. These events were used to help solidify lectures
topics that week. We spent a lot of time talking about Pyro Geography and Fire Science. This
year, Physical Geography had more hands-on labs than on the past. Students spent time working
with maps representing physical landscapes such as: alpine glaciers, karst topography, volcanic
landscapes and tectonic land features. Students also benefited from a series of labs designed
around a sand table simulator for interactive landscapes and fluvial activities that was introduced
this semester. This year I incorporated the use of compasses and mapping for field work. Students
enjoyed learning how to use these compasses and how to make a quick yet accurate field map.
Students also participated in an optional extra credit activity for a local environmental non-profit
to help clean-up our local watershed) The River Exchange Annual Great River Cleanup). This
activity corresponded with our weekly topic of watershed science, fluvial processes and
bioaccumulation of microplastics. Students were also exposed to professionals in the physical
science and geography profession with a series of guest speakers. These presentations focused
on topics and disciplines that we were being covered in class such as: geomorphology,
mineralogy, cartography, climatology and epidemiology. Guest speakers were used Skype and
Zoom to speak to my class from a variety of diverse research locations ranging from Antarctica
to Montana.
2. Discuss any support or obstacles encountered by the program.
One obstacle that was encountered this semester was not having direct access to the Physical
Geography and Earth Science store room. Not knowing what resources are available to this class
makes it hard to request them. If access is not granted it would be beneficial to have an inventory
of these resources made available.
4. Describe program budgetary needs or implications. (Attach completed CQIP form.)
A custom textbook and lab manual tailored to Physical Geography would be ideal for this class. I
have used these resources at other universities and it is a helpful way to select topics that pertain
to the college’s physical location. A custom lab manual would also allow for class resources to be
in one location while elevating lectures for additional activities. Another resource need is a globe.
This simple resource would be highly beneficial, and can be used for may lab activities and used
to teach about grids, scale and projections.
